question.content: The structure consists of two beams. The lower main beam AB is a simply supported beam with span l, with a pin support at A and a roller support at B. The upper auxiliary beam CD is also simply supported, and its two ends are supported at points C and D on the main beam, symmetrically arranged about the midspan of AB. The auxiliary beam length is a, and the distances from C and D to the adjacent main supports are both (l-a)/2. A downward concentrated load F acts at the midpoint of CD. This load is first carried by CD, then transferred to AB as two equal downward concentrated forces F/2 at C and D. Build the beam models for CD (midpoint concentrated load) and AB (two symmetric concentrated loads), and determine the reasonable auxiliary-beam length a by requiring both beams to reach bending strength simultaneously. Answer format: a single numerical value or algebraic expression; do not include units, explanatory text, or an equals sign.

step_1.content: Build two simplified beam models from the figure: (1) auxiliary beam CD as a simply supported beam with a midpoint load F; (2) main beam AB as a simply supported beam carrying two symmetric concentrated loads F/2 at C and D.
step_1.key_point: The load path is F on CD, then two F/2 transfers to AB.

step_3.content:
For the auxiliary beam CD with a midpoint load F:
R_C=R_D=\frac{F}{2}.
step_3.key_point: The midpoint point load on a simply supported beam gives equal end reactions.

step_4.key_point: Use standard max-moment results for a midpoint load and a symmetric two-point load.

step_5.content:
Set the two beams to reach bending strength at the same time:
\sigma_1=\sigma_2,
\frac{M_{\text{main,max}}}{W_1}=\frac{M_{\text{aux,max}}}{W_2}.
step_5.key_point: The design condition is equal maximum bending stress.

step_6.content:
Substitute moments and solve:
\frac{F(l-a)}{4W_1}=\frac{Fa}{4W_2}
\Rightarrow W_2(l-a)=W_1a
\Rightarrow W_2l=(W_1+W_2)a
\Rightarrow a=\frac{W_2l}{W_1+W_2}.
step_6.key_point: Final result: a is determined by section moduli ratio.
